articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Bolivar traces the deep roots of free Black life in Pennsylvania — free Negroes present by 1696, an early colored real-estate owner in Watson's Annals, and colored men voting in the state until 1835. He singles out Frederick Hinton as one of Philadelphia's most distinguished citizens, a leader in every movement for his people from 1831 until his death, and connects the era to David Walker's 1830 'Appeal' and to Robert Douglass's Annals of the early Philadelphia societies.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Rev. Theodore Gould
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A survey of the westward migration of Philadelphia's colored population out of the old 7th Ward wards east of Broad Street. Bolivar credits the Rev. Theodore Gould with first shaping this western exodus, founding Aden Chapel in 1873, after which Wesley, First Presbyterian, Grace Methodist and other churches followed their congregations west of Broad and south of Pine.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A meditation on the mission of the historian, arguing that the great denominations rest on the foundations laid by named pioneers: Richard Allen, Daniel Coker, Christopher Rush and James Varick for the Methodists; Absalom Jones for the Episcopalians; John Gloucester and John Chavis for the Presbyterians; and Lott Cary for the Baptists. Bolivar urges the young to draw their history from these forebears rather than rest on ancestral reputation.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: 75th anniversary of the Institute for Colored Youth
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: An account of the Institute for Colored Youth's 75th anniversary, commemorated at the Friends' Meeting House on 12th above Chestnut with Booker T. Washington present. Bolivar sketches the school's history from the Humphrey bequest through an 1849 experiment where Ishmael Locke taught, to the 1853 Lombard Street building under principal Prof. Charles L. Reason, with teachers Sarah M. Douglass and Grace M. Mapps (the first colored woman to graduate from a normal school).

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Prompted by the tensions of 1914, Bolivar looks back some fifty-three years to the firing on Fort Sumter and how Philadelphia's colored people absorbed the news at home, church, and every gathering place, sensing they would reap the benefit or suffer most from the war's outcome. He recalls the five-cent papers of the day — the North American under McMichael, the Press under Forney, and the Ledger under Swain and Abell.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Bolivar contrasts Philadelphia's wealth of memory with its scarcity of public monuments to Black achievement, citing the Shaw / 54th Massachusetts memorial in Boston and the Frederick Douglass statue in Rochester as the rare public honors. He argues that Deborah Gannett, the Black woman soldier of Revolutionary times, deserves substantial commemoration alongside figures like Molly Pitcher and Lydia Darragh.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Jefferson Row and the Fortens
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: On the historic block once called Jefferson Row, Bolivar notes claims that the Republican party was born in Sheriff William B. Elliott's residence, and that John G. Whittier lodged there while editing an anti-slavery paper. Whittier came to Philadelphia in 1831 for the initial anti-slavery convention and, befriending James Forten, was often a guest in his home, where he wrote in the album of the three accomplished Forten sisters.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: white champions of the anti-slavery cause
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Responding to a newspaper article on antebellum prejudice and the segregation of colored people from the street cars (and the lukewarm stance of Mayor Henry), Bolivar insists the opposition was not unanimous. He names the valiant white champions of the downtrodden — the lawyer David Paul Brown, who served without fee at cost to his purse and social standing, along with Edward Hopper, Henry Wharton, George Earle and William S. Pearce.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Rev. David Smith and the founding of the A.M.E. Church
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Reviewing the autobiography of Rev. David Smith, a pioneer minister of the A.M.E. Church, Bolivar illuminates the formative period around the denomination's first conference in 1816 and its coming centennial in 1916. He corrects Smith's claim that Bishop White consecrated Richard Allen: Allen was elected and the nearest approach to consecration was the laying on of hands by the Rev. Absalom Jones, with Daniel Coker also elected.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A column on three closely associated characters of the past whose names and celebrated deeds Bolivar treats as inseparable. (The clipping's OCR is badly degraded, so the specific individuals are not legible in this scan.)

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A short, poorly-scanned column touching on the St. Peter Claver (Catholic) congregation around Fifteenth Street and mentioning Andrew F. Stevens among those who later took up its leadership. (OCR quality is low for this clipping.)

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Dr. E. C. Howard
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Occasioned by the death of Dr. E. C. Howard, who had practiced medicine in Philadelphia for forty-three years, Bolivar reflects on the transition-era of the colored people just after the Civil War and the sympathy and legislative and personal efforts toward their betterment. He also references the earlier physician Dr. James H. (Wilson).

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: old colored newspaper days
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Continuing his history of old colored newspaper days, Bolivar recounts a Sunday Journal voting contest for the most popular man in the Gray Invincibles Battalion, its officer's-sword trophy won by James H. Davis over the battalion commander Captain Andrew F. Stevens. He praises the paper's owner-editor Robert G. Still and reproduces a striking portrait of Andrew F. Stevens, Sr.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Looking ahead to the 1916 centennial of Methodism, Bolivar urges that every salient feature of the A.M.E. communion — its million devotees and sixteen bishops — be set forth, and laments how the young see only the great churches, publishing houses and schools without knowing the sacrifices behind them. He names Philadelphia as the cradle and center of first Methodism across New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware and Maryland.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A reflection on the opening of the Civil War, when few dreamed it would last four years or end slavery, and when the navy had colored sailors but the army was wholly white under the cry 'Save the Union!' Bolivar describes wartime currency inflation and the impressment of slaves to garrison duty, and how the enlistment of Black men followed as the conflict deepened.
